ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'bread roll' class (FOODON_00001183). No specific entry for Italian saccoccio stuffed bread rolls exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and rolls as a category. No specific saccoccio entry exists; closest_parent category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 'Bread roll' is the closest defensible parent concept for saccoccio stuffed bread rolls.

main_ingredients: filling ingredients (variable: cheese, cured meats, or vegetables)

serving.natural_language_portion: One saccoccio bread roll weighing approximately 80 g, as typically served at Ricciotti restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bread, Italian, commercially prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 18033); supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods entry for enriched white bread rolls
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Saccoccio is a stuffed Italian bread roll made from enriched wheat dough. No exact USDA entry exists for saccoccio. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 18033 (Italian bread, commercially prepared) as the closest defensible analogue for the bread component. The stuffed filling (typically cheese and/or cured meats) adds fat and protein; values reflect a moderate-filling estimate consistent with USDA composite bread-roll data. Energy and macros are cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 325871 (bread rolls, plain). Adaptation is documented.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; White bread GI reference values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for saccoccio stuffed bread rolls. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread rolls and Italian white bread (GI range 70–75, mean ~72). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 39.4 g carbohydrate in 80 g serving) / 100 = 28.3. Available carbohydrate per serving = 49.2 g/100g × 0.80 × 100 = 39.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~490 mg per 100 g; ~392 mg per 80 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit saccoccio intake to one small roll per meal, as the sodium content from bread dough and any cured-meat filling can contribute meaningfully toward the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and high glycemic load per serving (~28.3) from refined wheat flour base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume saccoccio in small portions and pair it with protein or fat-rich foods to blunt the postprandial glucose response, given its high GI of approximately 72 and a glycemic load of ~28 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate content and variable saturated fat from filling ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, saccoccio should be consumed occasionally and in small portions, as the refined flour base contributes to glycemic burden and fillings containing cheese or cured meats may add saturated fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saccoccio (plural: saccocce) is a traditional Italian stuffed bread roll whose name derives from the Italian word for 'pocket' or 'pouch'. The bread is made from a leavened wheat dough — typically enriched with olive oil — that is shaped into individual rolls, filled with savoury ingredients such as mozzarella, prosciutto, salami, or roasted vegetables, then baked until golden. The result is a soft, slightly chewy bread exterior encasing a warm, flavourful filling. At Ricciotti restaurant in Singapore, saccoccio is presented as a selection of homemade breads and serves as an antipasto or accompaniment course. Nutritionally, saccoccio is a moderate-to-high calorie grain-based food providing approximately 271 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (~49 g/100 g) from refined wheat flour. Protein (~9 g/100 g) comes from both the dough and filling. The sodium content (~490 mg/100 g) is clinically relevant for hypertension management. The high glycemic index (~72) makes portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The bread is a source of B vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, folate) due to flour enrichment, and provides moderate iron and selenium. It is not suitable for those with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance.
common_user_queries: What does saccoccio mean in Italian?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for saccoccio exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy Italian bread and bread roll data (closest defensible analogue). GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white bread roll values. Confidence is medium because the filling composition at Ricciotti is variable and not publicly disclosed in a nutritional database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 18033 (Italian bread) and USDA FDC ID 325871 (plain bread rolls) as the closest approved analogues. No exact saccoccio entry exists in USDA, HPB Singapore Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. 2. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white bread roll values (GI range 70–75). 3. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy Italian bread entry. 4. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy Italian bread entry. 5. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy Italian bread entry. 6. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy Italian bread entry. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for Italian bread; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation. 8. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data; bread contains no ascorbic acid. 9. This is an Italian cuisine dish served in Singapore; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Italian cuisine requires no mandatory local-language names beyond the canonical English name and Italian romanized form. 10. Filling composition at Ricciotti is variable; sodium and fat values may be higher if cured meats are included. 11. Price data (SGD 8.72) preserved from input source for reference only; not used as nutritional evidence.
